---
title: Cache Overview
description: L1/L2/query caching with tags, eviction, and an analyzer in MountSQLI.
---

`@mountsqli/cache` caches query results to cut database load. It has a two-layer
store (L1 in-process, L2 shared), tag-based invalidation, and an analyzer that
decides what's safe to cache.

## Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
  Q[Query] --> B[CacheBridge]
  B --> L1[L1: in-process MemoryCache]
  B --> L2[L2: shared (e.g. RedisCacheDriver)]
```

- **L1** — fastest, per-process (`MemoryCache`).
- **L2** — shared across instances (`RedisCacheDriver`).

## The Bridge

`CacheBridge` sits between your queries and the `CacheManager`. On a read it
serves cached rows; on a write it invalidates by table tag.

```ts
import { CacheBridge } from "@mountsqli/cache";

const bridge = new CacheBridge(manager);
const { rows, fromCache } = await bridge.query(db, plan);
// after a write: await bridge.invalidateAfterWrite(plan);
```

## What gets cached

The `QueryCacheAnalyzer` inspects a `QueryPlan` and decides cacheability (e.g.
`SELECT`s are cacheable; writes are not). Cache keys are built from the plan via
`buildCacheKey`.

## Best practices

- Use the bridge for read-heavy endpoints.
- Let writes flow through `invalidateAfterWrite` so caches stay correct.
- Put L2 (Redis) in front of L1 for multi-instance apps.

## Common mistakes

- Caching writes or non-deterministic queries.
- Forgetting to invalidate on write — stale reads.

## Related

- [Eviction Policies](/cache/eviction/) — LRU/LFU/FIFO.
- [Tags & Invalidation](/cache/tags/) — invalidate by table.
